headline: Kampung Baru Master Redevelopment Remains Unrealised; PKB Has Delivered Smaller Projects
context: The Kampung Baru master redevelopment remains unrealised after four decades. PKB lists completed Kampong Bharu-area projects, but the master plan has not been delivered. The separate Kampung Sungai Baru sub-project (328 owners) moved forward in 2025.

cards.big: Forty years of proposals, no master redevelopment delivered. PKB lists completed smaller projects; the comprehensive plan has not begun.
cards.sub: PKB's operational area covers about 121.7 hectares (300.73 acres). PKB's 2010 census recorded 18,372 residents on the Malay Agricultural Settlement land.

cards.big: Kampung Baru sits beside the KLCC core. The land has Malay Agricultural Settlement status with Malay-only ownership restrictions.
cards.sub: PKB cites Malay-only ownership, multiple ownership, small lot sizes, ancestral-property issues and infrastructure obligations as combined constraints.

cards.big: PKB's 2010 census recorded 18,372 residents. Many trace household ties to the 1899 founding of the settlement.
cards.sub: Heritage value is officially recognised. PKB plans envisage phased redevelopment with Malay ownership safeguards and heritage protections built into design.

cards.big: PKB (Perbadanan Pembangunan Kampong Bharu), set up in 2012, has delivered specific projects but not the comprehensive master redevelopment.
cards.sub: The constraints PKB lists include Malay-only ownership, fragmented lots, ancestral property complications and infrastructure obligations.

cards.big: Kampung Baru is not a real estate problem. It is the unresolved question of whether Malay heritage and KLCC-grade land economics can coexist on the same blocks.
Like a historic neighbourhood where the heirs and the city disagree on renovation: four decades of blueprints, only patchwork delivery.
cards.sub: Kg Sungai Baru moved through a mix of acquisition, compensation, court processes and government intervention — not consensus among 328 owners.

cards.big: The path forward is not a bigger plan. It is structured smaller-block consolidation with heritage and Malay-ownership protections built into each phase.
